decision: Pure bash only
why: Zero install friction across macOS/Linux; no npm/venv/runtime; agents can modify the tool with just a text editor
alternatives: Node.js (install burden + adds drift), Python (venv pain), Go (compile step kills rapid iteration)

decision: Target bash 3.2 (macOS default)
why: macOS ships 3.2 and Apple will never upgrade it; requiring bash 5 would break install on every Mac
alternatives: bash 4+ features (associative arrays, `${var^^}`, `mapfile`)

decision: Markdown + YAML frontmatter
why: Human-readable, diff-friendly, loadable by any LLM, queryable by frontmatter_value(); JSON would require jq dep
alternatives: JSON (needs jq), SQLite-only (opaque to LLMs), custom binary (hostile to everything)

decision: LLM fills .platform/ at activation, tool never pre-picks stack
why: Static stack templates rot; the LLM reading actual code produces correct output the first time
alternatives: Shipping React/Django/etc. templates (would need per-stack maintenance + always wrong)

decision: ~300 lines per bash file as soft cap
why: Keeps files single-purpose; forces splits when commands accrete logic
alternatives: Hard linter enforcement (too rigid, we already violate on usage.sh/streams.sh where coupling justifies it)

decision: Claude Code PreToolUse hooks for commits + destructive ops; honor system for Codex/Gemini
why: Hard enforcement only where CLI exposes hook API; cross-CLI commit guarding would require shell shims (invasive)
alternatives: Git pre-commit hooks (fire on commits not on agent actions), PATH shims (invasive, fragile across shells)

decision: `.platform/memory/` folder with 7 append-only files + marker-bounded sections
why: Single location for durable knowledge; markers let `brief`/`close` parse reliably; bounded growth via log trim-to-10
alternatives: Database (opaque), per-stream memory (doesn't compound), unstructured files (can't be parsed for `brief`)

decision: SQLite at ~/.agentboard/usage.db, cumulative-delta mode on log
why: SQLite is on every modern OS by default; cumulative mode prevents double-counting when LLMs report running totals
alternatives: Flat JSON file (slow at scale), OpenTelemetry (overkill), delta-only mode (forces agents to track deltas manually)

decision: Agentboard runs on its own repo with `.platform/` gitignored
why: Real usage data > theoretical design; bugs surface via our own workflow; streams track our own features
alternatives: Not dogfooding (we'd keep shipping theater), committing .platform (pollutes the kit repo with instance state)

decision: Use launchd (macOS) + systemd user timer (Linux), not cron
why: Cron requires crontab-merge (clobber risk); launchd/systemd coalesce missed runs natively; user-level avoids sudo
alternatives: Cron (merge risk), nohup (no reboot survival), system-wide units (requires sudo)

decision: Rank changed files before auto-checkpointing; prefer stream-relevant files over lexically early scaffolding noise
why: Raw `git status` order surfaces `.claude/skills/*` before actual work files; ranking makes auto-checkpoints meaningful
alternatives: No ranking (produces misleading summaries), semantic diff analysis (out of scope)

decision: Semantic task types (`design`, `implementation`, `debug`, `audit`, `handoff`) logged separately from complexity labels
why: Generic labels (normal/heavy/trivial) hide spend root causes; task types reveal where tokens actually went
alternatives: Complexity only (masks the why), per-message telemetry (out of scope)

decision: Persist session-id → stream-slug mapping in `.platform/.session-streams.tsv` (tab-delimited, one row per session)
why: Lets event-logger and wrappers attribute events to the right stream across tool calls without passing context explicitly; TSV is shell-parseable with no deps
alternatives: In-memory only (lost on restart), SQLite (adds dep), env var only (breaks when multiple streams are open)

decision: `events.jsonl` captures only code-file edits (Edit/Write/MultiEdit to non-.platform/ files), git commits/pushes, and Reason annotations — all other tool events dropped
why: The log's job is provider handoff ("here's what I changed and why"), not a full audit trail; noise (Read, Bash, .platform/ meta-writes) obscures signal and bloats the handoff view
alternatives: Full PostToolUse capture (too noisy), manual-only logging (agents forget), separate noise/signal logs (doubles the surface)

decision: Canonical stream resolution must ignore stale env/brief state unless it still resolves to a real stream file, and callers should fall through to active context instead of failing hard on dead references
why: Closed streams routinely linger in `AGENTBOARD_STREAM`, session maps, and `BRIEF.md`; treating stale state as fatal poisons handoff, logging, and startup attribution
alternatives: Trusting env/brief blindly (misattributes work), failing resolution on dead state (breaks active inference), duplicating ad hoc fallback logic in each caller

decision: File locks are owned by session identity, not provider identity; provider remains descriptive metadata only
why: Same-provider concurrency is normal in multi-agent work. Provider-scoped locks let a second Codex or Claude session bypass the queue and falsely appear idempotent
alternatives: Provider-only ownership (collides across sessions), per-provider shared locks (can’t model queueing correctly), no session IDs in hooks/CLI (breaks release correctness)

decision: Non-Claude file-change pollers share persisted per-file diff fingerprints in `.platform/.file-change-state.tsv`, and emit a new row only when that fingerprint changes
why: In-memory filename sets only dedupe inside one poller process; concurrent wrappers and restarted pollers replay the same dirty snapshot and pollute the event log
alternatives: Per-process memory only (replays across wrappers), event-log post-processing (loses real-time signal), daemon-only dedup (breaks fallback path)

decision: Every new stream requires scaled research, targeted external research, a research-backed phased plan, and explicit human approval before implementation
why: New streams are where vague requirements and premature code create the most downstream churn; research + approval keeps Codex, Claude, and Gemini aligned before side effects
alternatives: Research only for medium+ streams (misses small-but-ambiguous work), provider-specific rules (drift), implementation before approval (weak human-in-loop)

decision: Stage 6 must include a structured manual QA plan whenever human behavior verification is relevant, or explicitly state why manual QA is not required
why: Developers and QA testers need repeatable click-through steps, expected results, data, environment, and regression checks after agent-built work; making it a workflow contract prevents vague "please test it" handoffs
alternatives: Ad hoc final notes (agents forget), ab-qa only for UI changes (misses backend/debug flows needing manual repro), automated tests only (does not guide human acceptance testing)

decision: Feature and bugfix streams must run from isolated worktrees branched from `develop`; hotfix streams use `hotfix/*` from `master` only when explicitly requested; each touched repo records dependency install status, local command, and localhost port(s) before coding
why: Multi-repo projects often have concurrent backend/frontend/mobile/MCP work; separate filesystems prevent branch collisions and local env ambiguity before implementation or QA
alternatives: Shared checkout work (mixes unrelated changes), current-branch defaults (can fork from wrong base), deferring dependency/port discovery until QA (creates late blockers)

decision: Roles are matched by intent, not keyword; `pair-programmer` is the silent fallback with no announcement; INDEX.md is the single routing source
why: Keyword matching breaks on non-native English and brief phrasing; a fallback role prevents silent failure when no match is found
alternatives: Keyword matching (too brittle), no fallback (silent failure), multi-role simultaneous activation (confuses model identity)

decision: Every `agent()` call in a workflow script must carry an explicit `model:` param; omission is treated as a bug, not a default; ab-workflow hard rule #10 requires a pre-flight self-audit before every `Workflow()` call
why: Omitting `model` inherits the caller's tier — a 10-agent fan-out on Opus costs 10× Sonnet for no quality gain on research/review/test-writing tasks
alternatives: Ambient guidance only (too easy to miss), session-level model lock (can't mix tiers within one workflow), post-run cost reporting only (closes the loop too late)

decision: Broad cleanup requests route to `code-cleanup-engineer` using `ab-cleanup`; deep architecture restructures remain `refactor-architect`, read-only assessments remain `code-auditor`, and measured performance investigations remain `perf-engineer`
why: "Clean up this codebase/path" spans duplication, dead code, oversized files, comments, generated artifacts, and low-risk maintainability work; folding it into refactor architecture would either over-scope simple housekeeping or encourage unsafe rewrites
alternatives: Use `refactor-architect` for all cleanup (too architectural), use `code-auditor` then handoff every time (read-only friction), add root-entry prose only (not reusable or testable)

topic: QA self-heal role and skill split
decision: Agent-driven app QA with Maestro/browser/project runners routes to `qa-automation-engineer` using `ab-qa-self-heal`; read-only readiness assessment remains `qa-engineer` with `ab-qa`
why: Self-healing QA fixes defects and reruns evidence loops, which conflicts with the independence constraints of the existing QA role; separating them keeps report-only QA clean while allowing bounded automation/fix loops with explicit safety gates
alternatives: Fold into `qa-engineer` (breaks read-only constraint), make Maestro mandatory (adds stack-specific dependency), document only in root entries (not reusable or testable)

decision: PM and engineering agents must apply a best-in-class, Silicon Valley-style product mindset across product and technical work, while preserving scope discipline, tests, maintainability, rollback thinking, and human approval gates
why: The owner wants future work to push beyond basic task completion toward innovative, user-obsessed, craft-driven, future-facing product quality; encoding it in workflow, roles, and entry templates makes it provider-neutral and repeatable
alternatives: Informal chat reminder only (lost across sessions), root-entry prose only (misses role behavior), unbounded ambition language (encourages scope creep)

decision: Manual QA now requires a durable stream-scoped markdown artifact at `.platform/work/qa/<stream-slug>-manual-qa.md` before commit, push, merge, release, or stream closure when human/app-driving verification matters; closed-stream QA artifacts move to `.platform/work/archive/qa/` and are never deleted
why: Chat-only QA plans disappear from handoffs and cannot reliably guide human QA or Maestro-style app-driving agents; preserving archived QA manuals creates regression references while keeping active work clean
alternatives: Chat-only final response plans (lost), repo-root `MANUAL_QA.md`/`QA_STEPS.md` assumptions (not every project has them), deleting QA docs at closure (loses regression evidence), leaving all QA docs in active work forever (stale active context)

decision: LLM-driven interactive QA with Maestro, Browser, Playwright, MCP, simulators, or similar tools must create `.platform/work/qa/<stream-slug>-execution-journal.md` documenting chronological actions, observations, expected/actual results, evidence, bugs, fixes, retests, successful paths, human blockers, and remaining risk
why: Manual QA artifacts describe the intended checklist, but they do not show what the agent actually did or learned while driving the app; the journal creates visibility into QA pipeline behavior and preserves both failures and successful paths for future debugging and learning
alternatives: Final summary only (hides sequence and missed steps), adding notes only to Manual QA artifact (mixes plan and execution), relying on tool transcripts (not durable or curated), requiring a journal for non-interactive shell-only verification (noise)

decision: Extension reads/writes a global `~/.agentboard/live.json` written by the Claude Code event-logger hook; the webview polls this file via a Node fs.watch watcher and posts updates via postMessage to prevent tab resets on data change
why: Workspace-local files break when VS Code opens a folder that is not the project root; a single global file lets the extension show live state regardless of which folder is active in VS Code
alternatives: Workspace-local live file (breaks multi-window), WebSocket server (adds always-on port dep), VS Code extension API storage (not writable by Claude hooks)

decision: All values interpolated into dashboard.js webview HTML must pass through `esc()` (HTML-entity encode); CSP img-src locked to `vscode-resource:` and `https:`; `openStream` validates path stays within workspace before calling `vscode.open`; `fmtModel` and `relTime` are null-safe
why: Webviews execute in a sandboxed browser context but XSS is still possible via injected stream/agent names; path traversal on `openStream` could open arbitrary files
alternatives: Trusting data from live.json as pre-sanitized (wrong — it is agent-written), blocking all external images in CSP (breaks legitimate badge/logo use)
